themes.name: Dust & Dynasties
themes.tagline: From gladiator uprisings to mango propaganda — history is always weirder than you think.

items.title: Mangoes, Madness, and Chairman Mao
items.channel_title: The History Guy: History Deserves to Be Remembered
items.description:
The history of Mangoes includes piracy, passion, paisley and a very unfortunate dentist. 

Check out our new shop for fun The History Guy merchandise:
https://thehistoryguy-shop.fourthwall.com/products/thg-history

This is original content based on research by The History Guy. Images in the Public Domain are carefully selected and provide illustration.  As very few images of the actual event are available in the Public Domain, images of similar objects and events are used for illustration. 

Not

items.title: This Is Spartacus!
items.description:
In 73 BC, a gladiator escaped slavery and launched a rebellion that shook the Roman Republic to its core. His name was Spartacus, and his uprising became one of the greatest threats Rome had ever faced from within.

Tristan Hughes is joined by Ben Kane to explore the dramatic story of Spartacus and his slave revolt that swept across Italy. Together, they cover the breakout at Capua to the battles against Roman armies and discover how Spartacus transformed a small band of fugitives into a force c

items.title: Why Did Henry VIII Betray Cardinal Wolsey? | Henry VIII's First Minister
items.description:
Cardinal Thomas Wolsey was merely the son of a butcher from Ipswich, yet he rose to become one of the most powerful men in Tudor England as Lord Chancellor to Henry VIII. He was even known as ‘The Other King’, and built a palace to rival Henry’s - Hampton Court. 

In this first episode of a new series on the Historic Royal Palaces Podcast, Tracy Borman is joined by historian Elizabeth Norton in the Great Hall at Hampton Court Palace. They explore Wolsey’s rise to power, the Reformation, Anne Bol

items.title: The Oldest Cola In The World is Not Coke
items.description:
Before the Coca-Cola vs. Pepsi cola wars took over the world, another soda was already making history.  On Colombia’s Caribbean coast, a mysterious bright pink drink was bubbling into existence decades before the big soda brands battled for dominance, and it’s still popular today.

On this episode of Weird History Food, we dive into the forgotten origins of Colombia’s iconic pink soda, Kola Roman, including the surprising story behind its invention, and how it survived the global takeover of the

themes.name: Rabbit Holes Worth Falling Down
themes.tagline: One daring prison break, a stolen attention span, and a cult horror film — buckle up.

items.title: You didn't lose your attention span. It was stolen.
items.description:
We're all worried about declining attention spans, but we forget something important: we didn't lose our attention spans, they were stolen. By looking at the history of newspapers, and then looking at how marketing works in the 21st century, we can see what we're up against.

Read my newsletter: https://jaredhenderson.substack.com

Watch my videos early on Nebula: https://go.nebula.tv/jaredhenderson

→Sources

The Attention Merchants by Tim Wu

'Who's Getting Rich Off Your Attention?' by Kyla Sc

items.title: KEEPER EXPLAINED: Every Symbol, Theme, and the Ending
items.description:
In this NEON Osgood Perkins Keeper 2025 Explained / Keeper Ending Explained and Analysis, I deep-dive analyze the Keeper movie starring Tatiana Maslany and Rossif Sutherland. Directed by Oz Perkins, the film is a perfect blend of mystery, horror, and psychological thriller, exploring themes of romantic relationships and emotional abuse. I believe this film is excellent in capturing the major themes of self-empowerment and self-love through great writing, acting, directing and filmmaking.

Furthe

items.title: Wildlife photos are mostly bad
items.description:
Go to http://squarespace.com/Chelsea & save 10% off your first website or domain with code “Chelsea"

Photographers forget that we only see other people best work! The best photos are often made after a series of failures. Follow Chelsea Mercado as she tracks down a wood stork and shares a series of photo tips and processes that help turn her failures into good wildlife photos.

morning_soundtrack.title: Neil Young Covers Series Heart Of Gold Adds MJ Lenderman, Kurt Vile, Ben Gibbard, & More: Hear King Princess & Draco Rosa’s Tracks
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Neil Young deserves more than one tribute album. He has written too many great songs and made too much of an impact, and one compilations of Neil Young covers won't do the trick, no matter how impressive it may be. Last year's Heart Of Gold: The Songs Of Neil Young Volume I featured people like Fion
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like flipping through a beloved songwriter's worn lyric notebook with a room full of friends who all dog-eared different pages.

morning_soundtrack.title: FOUNDERS KEEPERS: Margo Price Sings Truth to Power, Plus New Music from Luluc, Mason Jennings and More
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Here’s a challenge to all Americana artists: Start recording and releasing sociopolitical songs NOW (if you haven’t already). Write new songs if you’re good at that, but if not, there’s plenty out there that could always use a wider airing. As Margo
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sound of boots on pavement and a clenched jaw — music that demands you feel something and then do something about it.

morning_soundtrack.title: ALBUM REVIEW: Creekbed Carter Hogan Is a Court Jester of the Apocalypse on 'Peasants Revolt'
morning_soundtrack.snippet: “I hear it’s warmer than it ought to be / up there at the start of the Mississippi,” Creekbed Carter Hogan sings on “Burn.” “And the evergreens are like candle trees / they’re leaning into the burn.”The third track on Carter&
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Fiddle-and-brimstone folk for watching the world unravel with a dark laugh and a half-empty mason jar.

morning_soundtrack.title: SPOTLIGHT: John R. Miller - "Daughter of Night" [VIDEO]
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Editor's Note: John R. Miller, whose new LP titled The Great Unknowing came out July 17 via Rounder Records, is No Depression's Spotlight Artist for July 2026. Read more about Miller's fifth album here and stay tuned for
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Dusk settling over an empty two-lane highway, headlights not yet needed but the sky already turning.

morning_soundtrack.title: Nigel Godrich and Dhani Harrison Launch New Project Dragonflies
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Harrison, son of George, and Godrich will share their debut album in September
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The anticipation of a transmission arriving from somewhere between a Radiohead B-side and a Beatles dream sequence.

morning_soundtrack.title: MJ Lenderman, Kurt Vile, and Modest Mouse Cover Neil Young for New Compilation
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Listen to King Princess’ take on “Helpless” and Draco Rosa’s “After the Gold Rush” from Heart of Gold volumes two and three
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like hearing your favorite song through a neighbor's wall — familiar, slightly transformed, and somehow more tender for the distance.

morning_soundtrack.title: Nigel Godrich and Dhani Harrison Form Dragonflies, Release “Slower” Single: Stream
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Dragonflies' self-titled debut drops on September 18th.
Nigel Godrich and Dhani Harrison Form Dragonflies, Release “Slower” Single: Stream
Chris Coplan
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Celestial and unhurried, like watching light bend through water at the bottom of a very quiet afternoon.

morning_soundtrack.title: Jack White Testifies with Electrifying Catharsis on ‘Frozen Charlotte’
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Frozen Charlotte demonstrates once again that Jack White remains a high level blues rock maestro and tone scientist still in the prime of his career.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Amp-scorching and sweat-soaked, the kind of riff that makes you want to break something beautiful on purpose.

morning_soundtrack.title: Finn Wolfhard’s Sophomore Album Is an Off-the-Cuff Blockbuster
morning_soundtrack.snippet: On Fire From the Hip, Finn Wolfhard builds on the 1970s rock-inspired palette of his debut, modernizing the record with clever cultural allusions.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Basement-rehearsal energy that somehow stumbled into arena-worthy swagger, all flannel and feeling.

morning_soundtrack.title: Sequoia’s ‘So Long’ Is Imbued with Tuneful, Melodic Grace
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Sequoia's So Long is the sound of a well-lived, occasionally heartbroken folk troubadour who has seen it all and will happily oblige you with a story in song.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Sitting across from a weathered stranger at a roadside diner who starts talking and you miss your exit because you can't leave.

global_silver_linings.title: Rich Rainfall Set to Bust Mexico’s 6-year Drought
global_silver_linings.snippet: According to current predictions, Mexico may be free from a multi-year drought at the end of its current rainy season. Running from May to October, a wetter-than-normal El Niño phenomenon has led to rich rains across Mexico, from the drier north to the more humid south. Dante Sinohé Hernández Padrón, a researcher with the National […] The post Rich Rainfall Set to Bust Mexico’s 6-year Drought appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: After six years of grinding drought, meaningful rainfall returning to Mexico offers real relief for farmers, ecosystems, and communities who have been living with water scarcity. Nature's capacity to recover, given the chance, is one of the most grounding reasons for hope.

global_silver_linings.title: The rise of community-led football
global_silver_linings.snippet: As Premier League prices rise, growing numbers of supporters are seeking out something closer, cheaper and more human in non-league football The post The rise of community-led football appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: When supporters take ownership of their local clubs, football becomes about community and belonging rather than distant boardrooms — a quiet but powerful reminder that people can reshape institutions to serve them.

global_silver_linings.title: What We’re Reading: Saving One Centuries-Old Tree at a Time
global_silver_linings.snippet: Welcome back to our weekly behind-the-scenes glimpse at what’s getting our team talking. Tell us what you’ve been reading at info@reasonstobecheerful.world and we just might feature it here. Uprooted When trees are in the path of development, they have to go.
global_silver_linings.reason: Every ancient tree saved is centuries of living history preserved — and the dedicated people working tree by tree to protect them remind us that patient, careful stewardship still exists and still makes a difference.

discovery_articles.title: Leroy's Place New Orleans in New Orleans, Louisiana
discovery_articles.snippet: Stepping into Leroy's Place is like stepping through a portal into a whimsical monster world, right in the middle of the Historic French Quarter. A 10 foot tall, hot pink, puppet cat (named Casual Carl) greets you as you walk inside under huge fabric trees. And the surprises don't stop there!

discovery_articles.title: The Electrical Box Theatre in Los Angeles, California
discovery_articles.snippet: On the corner of Traction Avenue and 4th Place in Los Angeles’s Arts District sits a gray utility box. Big deal, right? Actually, yes.

discovery_articles.title: White Oak Avenue in Los Angeles, California
discovery_articles.snippet: Children of the 1980s will remember this street from the finale of this beloved classic, when the main character, Elliot, and his friends escape with E.T. from the clutches of government agents on bicycles and fly to safety. Officially filming in 1981, Steven Spielberg shot many scenes of this cherished movie in suburban areas of Los Angeles county, such as the San Fernando Valley.

discovery_articles.title: Drops of Rain in Étretat, France
discovery_articles.snippet: Situated within the Jardin Emotions of the Jardins d'Étretat, one can find the "Drops of Rain" sculptures by Samuel Salcedo, that pout, grin, and stick their tongues out at visitors. These sculptures are nestled into bushes in a way that is supposed to resemble oyster habitats, as an homage to Marie Antoinette's oyster farm by this garden. They display the range of human emotion with a sort of lightheartedness and absurdity that makes Étretat feel even more magical.

discovery_articles.title: Ancient Genes Reveal That Big, Bright Avocados Were Carried Through the Americas Thousands of Years Ago
discovery_articles.snippet: People brought large avocado seeds from South America to Central America, kicking off a burst of diversity for the fruit

discovery_articles.title: This 1950s Airplane Disaster Prompted the Flight Safety Demonstrations Passengers Know Today. Searchers Just Found the Wreckage in the Atlantic Ocean
discovery_articles.snippet: A team of explorers used an autonomous underwater vehicle to locate the remains of the Pan American ‘Clipper Endeavor’ about five miles off the coast of Puerto Rico

discovery_articles.title: Double Puggles: See the Adorable 'Twin' Echidnas Born at the San Diego Zoo Safari Park
discovery_articles.snippet: The two babies hatched earlier this year to one mother during the same breeding season—an extremely rare occurrence, according to the San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance

discovery_articles.title: A New World Record in the Greased Pole Walk Brought Extra Excitement to This Year's World Eskimo-Indian Olympics in Alaska
discovery_articles.snippet: A 45-year-old record fell at the annual sporting event, which celebrates Indigenous athleticism and culture with events including the knuckle hop and the Alaskan high kick

discovery_articles.title: A rare 3-D snake fossil is spilling secrets of its unusual lifestyle
discovery_articles.snippet: A new snake’s unique head anatomy suggests early snakes were even more diverse than thought. This one was a rare burrower.

discovery_articles.title: A newly found world blurs the boundary between planet and moon
discovery_articles.snippet: The object orbits a brown dwarf that orbits a star, and astronomers aren’t sure if it’s a planet, a moon — or something else entirely.

discovery_articles.title: Measles cases in the U.S. hit a new 34-year high
discovery_articles.snippet: The U.S. hasn't seen measles numbers like this since 1992. An epidemiologist explains what's driving the surge.

discovery_articles.title: This humpback whale hunting strategy has been out of sight — until now
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists documented humpback whales hunting together at the seafloor, coordinating their arrival to scoop up sand lances.

discovery_articles.title: Can cells think?
discovery_articles.snippet: Inside the lab of a biologist who is seeking to understand intelligence by focusing on problem-solving, not neurons - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: The Cassette Tapes of the Great Migration
discovery_articles.snippet: An archive of recorded interviews captures how ordinary people experienced one of the defining chapters of American history. The post The Cassette Tapes of the Great Migration appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

discovery_articles.title: The Era of Contradiction Materializes in Genesis Belanger’s Uncanny Sculptures
discovery_articles.snippet: “I’m interested in creating work that reflects the contradictions we navigate every day, where life can be both exhilarating and devastating." Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month. The article The Era of Contradiction Materializes in Genesis Belanger’s Uncanny Sculptures appeared first on Colossal .

featured_read.title: G.K. Chesterton on the Dandelion and the Meaning of Life
featured_read.snippet: Recovering the "forgotten blaze or burst of astonishment at our own existence" alive in the back of our modernity-deadened minds.
featured_read.blurb: Chesterton uses the humble dandelion as a crowbar to pry open the question of why familiarity quietly murders wonder — and whether we can get it back. This one asks you to look at something ordinary until it becomes strange again.

garden_note.note: Zürich's high summer evenings have that particular golden quality — the light lingering well past dinner, the linden blossom scent finally fading and giving way to the first warm whispers of ripening. Step outside tonight before the half-moon climbs too high and listen for the soft rustle of the bean canes; they've been busy all week.
in_season: Echinacea and rudbeckia coming into bloom
Deadheading roses for a late-summer flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: The First Quarter Moon hangs in the southwest after sunset — look just below it for Saturn rising steadily in the southeast as full dark settles in.
garden_note.sun_times: The sun slips away at 21:14 tonight, with dusk lingering a generous 35 minutes more until 21:49.

puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Anagram
puzzle.prompt: Rearrange the letters of SILENT to make a different common word.
puzzle.hint: There are 4 words hiding in these letters.

previous_puzzle.label: The Morning Haiku
previous_puzzle.prompt:
pap sips
his ____…
wheat-penny smooth

(Fill in the missing word.)

fern_data.greeting: Have you ever wondered how much of the world quietly lives without your noticing — a tree mostly dead wood and still standing, a whale hunting in ways no one caught on film until now, a cassette tape carrying an entire migration in its ribbon?
